title: N-Gram Language Models
learning_outcomes: Formulate MLE estimations for N-Gram language models under the Markov assumption.
Explain Laplace (add-one) smoothing and Kneser-Ney continuation distributions.

content_sections.title: N-Gram Probability Distributions
content_sections.content:
An N-Gram language model estimates the probability distribution of word sequences. By applying the chain rule of probability:

$$P(w_1, \dots, w_k) = \prod_{i=1}^k P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

Under the Markov assumption, the probability of the next word depends only on the preceding $N-1$ words:

$$P(w_i \mid w_1, \dots, w_{i-1}) \approx P(w_i \mid w_{i-N+1}, \dots, w_{i-1})$$

The Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE) is computed from corpus counts:

$$P_{MLE}(w_i \mid w_{i-1}) = \frac{C(w_{i-1}, w_i)}{C(w_{i-1})}$$

If the prefix $w_{i-1}$ never appeared in training, this estimate is undefined. If the prefix occurred but the pair $(w_{i-1}, w_i)$ did not, the probability is $0.0$, zeroing out the entire sequence probability.

content_sections.title: Smoothing Mechanisms: Laplace and Kneser-Ney
content_sections.content:
To resolve the zero-frequency problem, we apply smoothing:

1.  **Laplace (Add-One) Smoothing:** Adds 1 to all vocabulary counts. While simple, it shifts too much probability mass from observed words to unseen words in large vocabularies.
2.  **Kneser-Ney Smoothing:** An advanced smoothing technique. It estimates probabilities based on how likely a word is to complete an unseen context. Words that appear in many contexts (high continuation probability) are favored over words that have high individual frequency but only occur in specific contexts (e.g. 'Francisco' is frequent but almost exclusively follows 'San').
